answer:
**The poem is "Bilad-e-Islamiya" (The Abodes of Islam), chapter 2 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* by Allama Iqbal.**[^1]

This short Urdu nazm (verse poem) opens by invoking the sacred geography of Islam: Delhi ("Sarzameen Dilli"), with its dust holding "the blood of our forebears"; Baghdad as a site of Hijazi cultural flowering; Cordoba ("Qurtaba") as a beacon in Western darkness; Constantinople ("Qustuntinia") as a symbol of ummah power; and ultimately Yathrib/Medina ("Yasrab") as the Muslim's true homeland ("Des hai Muslim ka tu, maawa hai tu").[^2][^3]

The title phrase **"Bilad-e-Islamiya"** is a Perso-Arabic compound: *bilād* (Arabic: lands, countries, abodes) + *-e-Islamiya* (Persian/Urdu genitive of *Islāmiyya*, pertaining to Islam).[^4] By choosing this over a purely localized term (e.g., *Hind-e-Islamiya* for an Indian-Muslim frame or *subcontinent*-specific phrasing), Iqbal commits to a **pan-Islamic cosmopolitan frame of reference**. The poem surveys the historical *dar al-Islam* (abode of Islam)—from Hijaz heartlands, Abbasid Baghdad, Umayyad Andalusia, to Ottoman Istanbul—positioning these as a unified sacred network tied to the ummah's collective memory, sacrifice ("sainkdon sadiyon ki kisht-o-khoon"), and spiritual destiny. This transcends South Asian locality, rooting Muslim identity in transregional Islamic sovereignty (e.g., successors to Caesar and Jamshid) while elevating Medina as the devotional focal point ("Nuqta-e Jaazib-e-taasur"). The Arabic compound evokes classical Islamic geography (e.g., *bilād al-Islām* in medieval texts), binding the poem to a universalist, non-nationalist Islamic worldview amid colonial partitions.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 2: "Bilad-e-Islamiya" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/]).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-876603](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-876603)–[p-4ecfd3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-4ecfd3) (Delhi stanza).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-f92993](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-f92993)–[p-c3029f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c3029f) (Medina stanzas).
[^4]: Ibid., frontmatter/title: [p-3015c7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-3015c7) ("The Abodes of Islam"); cf. transliteration: [p-46e448](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/transliteration/#p-46e448)–[p-d9cd42](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/transliteration/#p-d9cd42) ("Bilad-e-Islamiya").
[^5]: Ibid., [p-630e3b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-630e3b) ("the very heart of the Islamic nation") and [p-00709d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-00709d)–[p-c6a6c2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c6a6c2) (global sovereignty).
